Name: pac-bayes_spectral_stability_inference
Title: PAC-Bayes Spectral Stability Bounds for Latency-Critical Inference
Short Hypothesis: By integrating PAC-Bayes theory with spectral stability metrics, we can derive strictly bounded-overhead protocols for latency-critical inference, minimizing hidden-state perturbation and enforcing strict wall-clock budgets.
Related Work: Related works include Spectral Quantization Routing and Training-Free Geometric Interference Cancellation, which focus on dynamic precision allocation and geometric interference cancellation, respectively. However, these works do not explicitly leverage PAC-Bayes theory to derive strictly bounded-overhead protocols for latency-critical inference. Our proposal distinguishes itself by integrating PAC-Bayes bounds with spectral stability metrics, providing a rigorous theoretical foundation for enforcing strict latency budgets.
Abstract: We propose a novel framework that integrates PAC-Bayes theory with spectral stability metrics to derive strictly bounded-overhead protocols for latency-critical inference. Our approach minimizes hidden-state perturbation and enforces strict wall-clock budgets by leveraging Jacobian-based layer-skipping frameworks. We hypothesize that spectral stability metrics, such as the local covariance condition number and spectral decay rate, serve as direct proxies for inference stability under perturbation. By deriving PAC-Bayes bounds linked to these metrics, we establish a mathematically transparent pathway for optimizing inference efficiency under strict latency constraints. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across adversarial benchmarks and natural domain shifts, our method achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier. We provide theoretical bounds linking spectral stability to generalization under perturbation, offering a reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Evaluate the impact of PAC-Bayes bounds on inference stability by measuring the perturbation-cost and memory-bandwidth accounting across different latency budgets.
Experiment 2: Validate the effectiveness of Jacobian-based layer-skipping frameworks by comparing the throughput-accuracy Pareto frontiers with and without layer-skipping.
Experiment 3: Assess the robustness of the proposed framework under structured perturbations and domain shifts, quantifying stability radii and generalization performance.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Risk 1: The computational overhead of computing spectral stability metrics on-the-fly may outweigh the benefits of enforcing strict latency budgets.
Risk 2: The proposed framework may not generalize well to tasks with highly dynamic input distributions, limiting its applicability in real-world scenarios.
Limitation 1: The method relies on single-GPU setups, and its scalability to multi-GPU environments remains untested.
Limitation 2: The theoretical bounds derived from PAC-Bayes theory may be too conservative, leading to suboptimal inference efficiency.
